description: VulnCheck provides vulnerability intelligence and exploit monitoring services for security teams.

build_patterns.name: Knowledge Graphs
evidence: VulnCheck generates Mitre mappings for CWE, CAPEC and Att&ck
mapping to GreyNoise tags
CPE strings
mitreAttackTechniques
configurations / vcConfigurations / vulnerableCPEs
relatedAttackPatterns
build_patterns.description: Strong evidence of rich entity linking and relationship indexing: CVEs, CPEs, CWEs, CAPEC, MITRE ATT&CK techniques, references, and IP/C2 fingerprints are normalized and cross-referenced. This indicates an internal graph-like model or graph database (permission-aware indexes/RBAC not explicit but entity relationships and mappings are central).

build_patterns.name: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
evidence: VulnCheck indices can be accessed using either the backup endpoint, which provides a complete copy of the index for download, or the index endpoint, which provides a paginated list of documents and can be used to query individual records
curl --request GET https://api.vulncheck.com/v3/index/vulncheck-nvd2?cve=CVE-2024-21762
VulnCheck packages these detection artifacts for organizations to detect & respond
leverages both publicly available systems for measuring exposure (e.g., Shodan) as well as VulnCheck scan infrastructure
build_patterns.description: The product exposes multiple indexed data sources and APIs for querying and retrieving context (CVE records, backups, rules). While explicit embedding/vector store mentions are absent, the architecture shows separation of retrieval indices and downstream synthesis/enrichment (temporal CVSS, SSVC), consistent with RAG-style systems combining stored knowledge with generation/enrichment pipelines.

build_patterns.name: Continuous-learning Flywheels
evidence: autonomous system that tracks hundreds of vendor and government advisories
real-time and historic tracking of honeypot detections are available via API
documentGenerationDate
VulnCheck Exploit and Vulnerability Intelligence is an autonomous system that tracks hundreds of OSINT sources
VulnCheck Canaries capture real-world attacker behaviors and exploitation techniques in the wild
build_patterns.description: There are clear feedback loops: canaries and honeypot telemetry feeding detections, continuous ingestion of OSINT/advisories, and automated inference (temporal CVSS, SSVC). This implies ongoing model/data updates and enrichment driven by new observations, constituting a continuous-learning pipeline.

build_patterns.name: Agentic Architectures
evidence: built from a fully autonomous system in software
autonomous system that tracks hundreds of OSINT sources
authors detection artifacts
VulnCheck authored scanners focused on detecting already-compromised implanted devices
VulnCheck packages these detection artifacts for organizations to detect & respond
build_patterns.description: Language in the content describes autonomous processes that discover, triage, author detection artifacts, and run scanners — suggesting orchestrated autonomous agents or workflows that use tools (scanners, canaries, API endpoints) to perform multi-step tasks rather than a single monolithic model.

build_patterns.name: Vertical Data Moats
evidence: VulnCheck's Initial Access Intelligence product provides organizations with dockerized in-house developed exploits
VulnCheck packages these detection artifacts
VulnCheck has authored and maintains go-exploit
VulnCheck's C2 denylists
VulnCheck Exploit & Vulnerability Intelligence replaces the need to correlate vulnerability intelligence across hundreds of sources
commercially-licensed products and community products
build_patterns.description: VulnCheck clearly builds proprietary, domain-specific assets (exploit PoCs, C2 fingerprints, curated indices, detection artifacts, custom CPEs and SSVC generation) that form an industry-specific dataset and tooling moat — a classic vertical data moat strategy.

build_patterns.name: Micro-model Meshes
evidence: fingerprints dozens of Command and Control (C2) attacker infrastructure types
leverages these fingerprints alongside existing Internet infrastructure mapping technologies
tracks hundreds of OSINT sources
build_patterns.description: There is indirect indication of many specialized components (scanner types, fingerprinting modules) that could be implemented as specialized models, but there is no explicit mention of multiple small ML models, model routing, or ensemble model orchestration.

build_patterns.name: Natural-Language-to-Code
evidence: authors detection artifacts
VulnCheck packages these detection artifacts
VulnCheck has open sourced go-exploit, an SDK for Go and CLI
build_patterns.description: The system programmatically generates rules and artifacts (Suricata/Snort rules, YARA, Nmap scripts). However, there's no explicit evidence of a natural-language interface that translates plain English into code/rules; generation appears data-driven rather than NL-driven.

build_patterns.name: Guardrail-as-LLM
evidence: VulnCheck IP Intelligence provides tracking of honeypots on the Internet, so that when we report on potentially vulnerable systems, we do not include counts of honeypots
real-time and historic tracking of honeypot detections are available via API
build_patterns.description: The product includes validation/cleanup mechanisms (honeypot filtering, denylist ingestion) which are guardrail-like, but there's no sign of a secondary model layer explicitly checking or moderating model outputs for safety or compliance.

sub_sub_vertical: SOC/NOC threat intel and vulnerability prioritization

signals: Open source go-exploit library authored and maintained by VulnCheck
Go SDK examples (sdk-go-v2) provided and used in code samples
Public API with v3 endpoints and backup/index/rules endpoints
Dockerfile provided for in-house exploits and scanners
Engineering blog presence via blog URL (e.g., automating-ssvc)

team_strengths: In-house exploit PoCs and network scanners written in Go with Dockerized deployment artifacts
Authored and maintains an OSS library (go-exploit), indicating core development capability and codebase ownership
Extensive detection artifacts: Go-based PoCs, Suricata/Snort signatures, YARA (where possible), CPE strings, Nmap scripts, and vulnerability mappings
Threat intelligence breadth with IP intelligence, C2 fingerprinting, honeypot tracking, and API-accessible datasets
team_red_flags: No disclosed founders' names, bios, or leadership details in the provided material
Lack of explicit information about company size, location, or active hiring signals

pricing_evidence: Free community products at no cost
Free plan with unit usage limit mentioned in content
Upgrade to a paid plan prompt and references to paid subscriptions
Paid subscription license required for Initial Access Intelligence

evidence: API-first posture with multiple endpoints (/v3), SDKs (Go SDK), and CLI samples
GitHub repositories and offline backups demonstrated in examples
Dockerized PoCs and in-house exploit tooling targeted at developers and security teams
Open source go-exploit library indicates a developer/community-led distribution
Free tier with upgrade path encourages self-serve adoption

revenue_model.monetization_approach: Hybrid SaaS plus OSS: commercial licenses for enterprise-ready detection/artifact suites and APIs, with a free/community tier for broad adoption; revenue tied to paid subscriptions for commercial products
unit_economics_signals: No explicit margins or ARPU data provided
Recurring subscription revenue implied via licenses and API access

distribution_advantages: Open-source go-exploit library and OSS ecosystem
Community products at no cost complementing commercial offerings
API access and SDKs enable easy integration into customer tooling
Dockerized artifacts and PoCs facilitate rapid deployment

acquisition_channels: self-serve sign-up with free tier and upgrade path

stage_evidence: Multiple product lines (Initial Access Intelligence, IP Intelligence, Exploit & Vulnerability Intelligence) with extensive feature sets
OSS go-exploit library and in-house PoC / network scanner code; Dockerized deployment examples
Go SDK v2 with API client usage examples; REST API endpoints for backups, rules, and indices
Community (open-source) and commercial licensing with enterprise-grade features like honeypots, canaries, C2 denylists
Integration with Shodan, Censys, GreyNoise, and threat intel workflows; ability to import into TIPs
REST API for automation, offline backups and encrypted backups; documented sample code

core_features: Initial Access Intelligence: in-house exploits PoCs, network scanners, PCAPs, Suricata/Snort signatures, YARA rules (when possible)
Vulnerability Intelligence: enrichment, CVSS, CPE enrichment, KEV/ NVD integration, risk scoring
IP Intelligence: C2 fingerprinting, honeypots, exposure measurements on internet, VPN/proxy detection
Export/delivery: API access, endpoints to fetch rules, backups, and vulnerability data
Canaries and honeypots to capture attacker behavior
differentiating_features: Couples exploit intelligence with vulnerability intelligence (not just vulnerability databases)
Integrated with external data sources (Shodan, Censys) to measure internet exposure
Live C2 denylists exportable to TIPs; threat intel workflow interoperability
Open-source go-exploit library and offline backups for artifact management
roadmap_signals: Presence of both OSS and commercial offerings suggests ongoing development
SDK v2 and CLI tooling indicate continued tooling expansion
Documentation and examples imply ongoing ecosystem maturation

integrations_mentioned: OSINT sources (NVD, CISA KEV, CERT advisories via VulnCheck indices)

use_cases.primary_use_case: Detect and respond to initial access/exploit vulnerabilities with in-house detection artifacts and internet exposure measurement
secondary_use_cases: Threat intelligence enrichment and vulnerability prioritization via Exploit & Vulnerability Intelligence
C2 infrastructure and honeypot monitoring and denylisting
Open-source intelligence-driven vulnerability analysis via API
industry_focus: ICS/OT environments (as indicated by vulnerability data)

product_risks: Dual-use risk: PoCs and exploit code could be misused if not properly controlled
Reliance on external data sources (Shodan, Censys) which may affect accuracy
Potential misinterpretation of honeypot counts due to internet-scale data variability
No explicit mention of compliance/regulatory alignment in provided content
product_strengths: Strong integration of exploit intelligence with vulnerability intelligence
Broad artifact portfolio (PoCs, PCAPs, IDS signatures, YARA, Nmap scripts)
Active OSINT-driven vulnerability tracking and exposure measurement
Programmable via API/SDK with offline backups and CLI tooling
Open-source go-exploit library enables customization and extension

unique_findings: They treat exploit proof-of-concepts as first-class, productionized artifacts: PoCs and network scanners are implemented in Go, packaged with Dockerfiles, and distributed via a git/backup API so customers can pull runnable exploit containers and accompanying detection artifacts. That pipeline (code -> container -> customer) is unusual compared to most vendors who publish only advisories or static PoCs.

Open-sourcing and maintaining a shared go-exploit library (SDK) to standardize exploit development. This creates a reproducible developer experience for exploit authors and enables consistent logging/telemetry across disparate PoCs — effectively productizing exploit engineering as a platform primitive.

End-to-end pairing of offensive and defensive artifacts: each vulnerability record is shipped with PoC code, network-level captures (PCAP), Suricata/Snort rules, YARA when possible, Nmap scripts, vulnerable Dockerfiles, and CPE mappings. Packaging all these artifact types together is rare and implies integrated toolchains for generating signatures from active exploits.

Active internet exposure measurement combined with honeypot de-duplication and C2 fingerprinting: instead of naive Shodan counts, they correlate Shodan/Censys results with honeypot detection heuristics and custom scanners to produce de-noised exposure metrics. That requires complex telemetry correlation and ongoing anti-noise modeling.

They generate their own 'VulnCheck CPEs' and vcConfigurations in addition to NVD CPEs, suggesting an automated CPE-generation engine that infers product/version fingerprints from heterogeneous advisories and evidence — a non-trivial normalization and entity-resolution problem.

Automated enrichment pipeline that fuses exploit intelligence, vendor advisories, CISA KEV, EPSS, CVSS temporal metrics, and generates SSVC-like decision metrics. This indicates an autonomous rules-and-data-driven scoring layer rather than just aggregation of external scores.

Operational focus on C2 detection and denylisting with mappings to GreyNoise and numerous implant families (Havoc, Sliver, Meterpreter), plus APIable denylist exports for TIPs — this is a practical productization of threat actor infrastructure tracking aimed at immediate operational blocking.

Security/operational oddity: docs reveal an offline encrypted backup labeled 'initial-access-git-encrypted' with an explicit password 'vulncheck' in the text. That indicates either sloppy documentation practices or an alarming potential operational security lapse; it weakens trust in the secure distribution claims.

story_angles.headline: Inside VulnCheck’s autonomous pipeline: dockerized Go PoCs, an OSS go-exploit SDK, and continuous vulnerability-to-detection feedback loops
story_angles.summary: VulnCheck appears to have built an end-to-end autonomous engineering stack that goes from advisory ingestion to generating exploit PoCs, hardened scanner code, detection signatures, and distribution via APIs and git backups. For builders, the interesting part is how they stitch together Dockerized Go exploits, an open-source go-exploit library, and telemetry (Shodan/Censys/Canaries) into a continuous-learning flywheel that can rapidly produce detection artifacts.
evidence: Exploit PoCs and scanners are written in Go and distributed with Dockerfiles (example compile/run log for CVE-2023-22527).
They maintain an open-source go-exploit shared library used by their PoCs.
Claims of an autonomous system that tracks hundreds of advisories and automatically marries them with exploit intelligence.

story_angles.headline: Vertical data moat: why VulnCheck’s C2 denylists, honeypot-aware exposure counts, and GreyNoise/SHODAN mapping matter more than CVE lists
story_angles.summary: VulnCheck emphasizes operationally useful data over raw vulnerability catalogs: live C2 fingerprints and denylists, honeypot detection to de-noise exposure metrics, mapping to GreyNoise tags, and Shodan/Censys-driven internet exposure measures. That combination creates a vertical data moat for defenders who need actionable signal rather than raw CVE noise.
evidence: They fingerprint dozens of C2 types (Havoc, Sliver, Meterpreter) and provide C2 denylists consumable by TIPs.
They explicitly track and filter honeypots when reporting potentially vulnerable systems and expose real-time/historic honeypot detections via API.
They map their detections to GreyNoise tags and use Shodan/Censys for Internet-level exposure measurements.

story_angles.headline: Built by operators: how exploit intelligence + vulnerability intelligence refocuses remediation for critical infrastructure and ICS/OT targets
story_angles.summary: Unlike generic vulnerability feeds, VulnCheck couples exploit telemetry and vendor advisories to prioritize remediation for high-risk verticals (ICS/OT, IoT). Their data model (vulncheck-nvd2) embeds CISA KEV dates, temporal CVSS, and domain-specific MITRE techniques—useful to teams defending industrial environments where exploitation risk differs from IT.
evidence: vulncheck-nvd2 entries include CISA KEV add dates, temporal CVSS fields, and tags like ICS/OT and IoT in categorization.
MITRE attack techniques in the dataset include domain-specific entries (e.g., T0819 Exploit Public-Facing Application in ICS domain).
They produce tailored scanners for certain vulnerabilities (e.g., PaperCut using Shodan queries, Cisco IOS XE using scanners for implanted devices).

story_angles.headline: They open-sourced their exploit SDK while commercializing detection — a deliberate bet that sharing PoC tools speeds defensive innovation
story_angles.summary: VulnCheck ships exploit PoCs and scanners commercially but has open-sourced go-exploit. That’s a contrarian trade-off: by releasing the SDK they accelerate community tooling and researcher uptake while keeping curated detection artifacts and operational denylists behind paid APIs — effectively crowdsourcing improvements while monetizing operational hardening.
evidence: The company open sourced go-exploit, an SDK for Go used by their exploit PoCs and CLI for API usage.
Commercial products include packaged detection artifacts (Suricata/Snort rules, YARA, C2 denylists) and paid API access to curated datasets.
Distribution mechanisms include Dockerized PoCs, git over SSH downloads, and API-backed rule endpoints — a hybrid open/commercial model.

anti_patterns.description: Much of the value described depends on aggregating public data (Shodan, Censys, NVD/CISA advisories), open rule formats (Suricata, Snort, YARA), and a shared open-source exploit library. Core components appear replicable or publicly available, which weakens defensibility.
evidence: Explicit use of public scanners and indexing services (Shodan, Censys)
Suricata/Snort rules and YARA signatures are standard, public formats served via simple API endpoints
go-exploit is open sourced and the SDK/CLI are available, reducing proprietary lock-in
Large parts of vulnerability intelligence (CVEs, CVSS, advisories) are sourced from public feeds and advisories

anti_patterns.description: The offering reads largely as a curated/packaged layer over existing data sources, tooling, and common formats rather than a fundamentally new technology stack — strong integration/packaging but limited proprietary innovation shown.
evidence: APIs for fetching rules/backups and examples showing simple curl/git/SDK usage imply aggregation and distribution
Dockerized PoCs and example make/docker/run flows wrap code and tooling for convenience
Many features (rule feeds, C2 denylist, backups) are distribution/packaging of artifacts rather than novel inference engines

anti_patterns.description: Several capabilities (rule feeds, C2 denylists, vulnerability enrichments) are discrete features that incumbents or SIEM/TIP vendors could incorporate into existing platforms, risking absorption rather than standalone differentiation.
evidence: C2 denylists described as easily importable into commercial TIPs
Suricata/Snort rule feeds and backup downloads are functionality many larger vendors already provide or can add
The Initial Access Intelligence focus is a narrow product line that could be bundled into broader security suites

anti_patterns.description: Marketing asserts 'best-in-class' and a 'fully autonomous system' with limited technical detail on how autonomy, model training, or unique telemetry are implemented — language risks overstating capability.
evidence: Phrases like 'best-in-class information' and 'fully autonomous system in software' appear without operational/architectural detail
Descriptions emphasize outputs (feeds, PoCs) more than describing unique data pipelines, models, or proprietary telemetry

anti_patterns.description: The vulnerability intelligence and exploit space is crowded; while the mix of artifacts is useful, the offering lacks an obvious, clearly defensible unique angle beyond curation and packaging.
evidence: Multiple vendors and open projects provide CVE enrichment, exploit PoCs, and IDS rules
Open-source components and community products reduce perceived uniqueness
Core functionality (vulnerability indexing, CVSS enrichment, rule distribution) is common across many competitors

competitors.name: Recorded Future
competitors.similarity: Provides broad threat & vulnerability intelligence, timelines, exploit / exploitability context, API access and integrations into security tooling.
competitors.how_different: VulnCheck emphasizes actionable detection artifacts (dockerized PoCs, Suricata/Snort rules, YARA, PCAPs) and has in-house exploit development and canary telemetry; Recorded Future focuses more on large-scale OSINT aggregation, analyst-driven reports, and enterprise risk scoring.

competitors.name: Tenable (including Tenable Lumin)
competitors.similarity: Vulnerability prioritization, CVE enrichment, integration into remediation workflows, focus on which vulnerabilities to fix first.
competitors.how_different: Tenable is primarily a vulnerability scanning and risk-management platform (integrated VM product). VulnCheck differentiates by coupling exploit PoCs, detection rules, C2 denylists, and internet-exposure scanning (Shodan/Censys) rather than asset-first scanning.

competitors.name: Rapid7 (InsightVM / Threat Command)
competitors.similarity: Vulnerability intelligence, exploit context, integration into detection and response workflows, and threat intelligence capabilities.
competitors.how_different: Rapid7 combines vulnerability management and threat intel into a platform. VulnCheck is API-first and delivers packaged detection artifacts (rules, PCAPs, dockerized exploits) and autonomous exploit-tracking specific to initial access and exploit weaponization.

competitors.name: Qualys
competitors.similarity: Vulnerability data, CVE enrichment, enterprise integrations, and vulnerability prioritization features.
competitors.how_different: Qualys is a broad IT security platform with asset discovery and scanning. VulnCheck prioritizes exploit telemetry, hands-on PoCs and detection content that defenders can directly deploy (e.g., Suricata/Snort/YARA), plus C2 denylists and canary telemetry.

competitors.name: Risk Based Security / VulnDB
competitors.similarity: Maintains a curated vulnerability database with enrichment and exploit/weaponization context; used for prioritization.
competitors.how_different: VulnCheck claims a more autonomous pipeline that explicitly marries exploit intelligence with vulnerability metadata and provides executable detection artifacts and scanner code (go-exploit), not just curated vulnerability records.

competitors.name: GreyNoise / Censys / Shodan (data providers / adjacent competitors)
competitors.similarity: Internet-wide scanning / fingerprinting and enrichment for IPs and services; GreyNoise provides noisy-actor context and Censys/Shodan provide exposure data.
competitors.how_different: VulnCheck integrates these signals but extends them by generating exploit PoCs, honeypot/canary telemetry, C2 denylists, and packaged detection signatures. VulnCheck is positioned as a product that consumes those sources and outputs deployable defender artifacts.

competitors.name: Exploit-DB / Metasploit / Public PoC repositories
competitors.similarity: Public exploit proof-of-concepts and exploit code that defenders and attackers use to test vulnerabilities.
competitors.how_different: VulnCheck authors and packages in-house exploit PoCs and provides them dockerized with Go-based implementations plus accompanied detection artifacts (rules, PCAPs), curated telemetry and maturity/timeline metadata — i.e., structured, signed & packaged for defender use rather than raw PoC dumps.

competitors.name: CrowdStrike / Mandiant (threat intelligence services)
competitors.similarity: Threat actor tracking, C2 detection, incident telemetry, and prioritized vulnerability exploitation insights.
competitors.how_different: These firms bundle threat intel with endpoint detection and response and incident response services. VulnCheck focuses on an autonomous, API-first exploit + vulnerability intelligence feed with deployable detection artifacts and a developer-oriented distribution model (git repos, API backups, SDKs).

differentiation.primary: Marrying hands‑on exploit intelligence (dockerized PoCs, go-exploit library, scanner code, PCAPs) with vulnerability enrichment and Internet exposure telemetry to produce immediately deployable detection artifacts (Suricata/Snort/YARA) and C2 denylists via an API-first, autonomous pipeline.
differentiation.technical: Key technical differentiators include: an autonomous ingestion and enrichment pipeline combining OSINT, vendor advisories, honeypot/canary telemetry and internet scanners; a Go-based exploit SDK (go-exploit) and dockerized PoC delivery model; API indices + SDK/CLI and offline backup endpoints; automated CPE generation, CVSS temporal metrics, automated SSVC decisions, and integration of Shodan/Censys/GreyNoise signals for exposure and noise filtering.
differentiation.business: API-first, feed-and-artifact distribution (git clone, backup downloads, paginated indices), freemium/community products plus commercial licensed indices, packaged detection artifacts for defenders (not just data), and tooling (SDK, CLI) that accelerates integration into existing detection, TIP and SIEM workflows.
differentiation.positioning: Positioned as a next‑generation cyber threat intelligence vendor that is exploit-centric and defender-focused: 'we don’t just tell you a vuln exists — we provide PoCs, detections and real-world exploitation telemetry so you can detect and respond faster' — contrasting with vendors that focus mainly on CVE aggregation or vulnerability scanning.

secret_sauce.core_advantage: A vertically integrated pipeline that authors, tests and packages exploit PoCs + detectors (rules/PCAPs/YARA), enriches them with telemetry (canaries, C2 fingerprints, Shodan/Censys exposure, EPSS/SSVC) and serves them via API/SDKs and downloadable repositories — turning raw exploit discovery into immediately actionable defensive artifacts.
secret_sauce.defensibility: Moderate: requires specialized offensive/defensive engineering skills, continuous investment in honeypots/canaries and scanning infrastructure, curated mappings and telemetry, and the operational processes to safely author/package PoCs and detection rules. These capabilities create time-to-market and trust barriers for newcomers, but larger TI vendors or open-source communities could replicate parts of the stack over time.
evidence: “in-house developed exploit PoCs and network scanners, packet captures (PCAP files), Suricata & Snort signatures… YARA rules… vulnerable docker files”
“The exploits leverage an Open Source Software (OSS) shared library… called go-exploit.”
“VulnCheck packages these detection artifacts for organizations to detect & respond.”
“VulnCheck is built from a fully autonomous system in software.”
“VulnCheck fingerprints dozens of Command and Control (C2) attacker infrastructure types… C2 denylists… may be easily imported into commercial TIPs and threat intelligence workflows.”
API-first distribution with SDKs/CLI and offline backups (git clone, /v3/backup/* endpoints) demonstrates a developer / integrator-focused delivery approach.

competitive_analysis.moat_explanation: VulnCheck’s moat is driven by a specialized, integrated capability set: continuous exploit development, packaging of PoCs into dockerized artifacts, curated detection signatures, canary/honeypot telemetry and automated enrichment (SSVC, CVSS temporal metrics, EPSS). These require sustained engineering and operational investment and provide defensible time‑to‑value for customers. However, major TI vendors, vulnerability database providers, or well‑resourced open‑source projects could incrementally replicate or absorb parts of the offering (feeds, rules, PoCs), and the underlying raw data (exploits, CVEs, Shodan/Censys scans) is often public or obtainable — making the moat meaningful but not impregnable.

evidence_quotes: The content discusses exploits, OSINT, APIs, Suricata/Snort rules, signatures, YARA rules, canaries, honeypots, and threat intel indices, but contains no references to language models, LLMs, GPT, Claude, embeddings, RAG, prompts, or any generative AI terminology.
Tight integration of exploit PoCs (dockerized Go exploits + go-exploit SDK) with vulnerability intelligence — combining executable PoCs, detection rules, and CVE enrichment in one product.
API design exposing both 'backup' (complete downloadable index) and paginated 'index' endpoints enabling offline distribution of full indices alongside live queryable datasets.
Automated generation of temporal CVSS and SSVC metrics from aggregated OSINT and exploit signals, plus generation of VulnCheck-specific CPEs (vcConfigurations) in addition to NVD CPEs.
Honeypot-aware exposure measurement: actively identifying and excluding honeypots from Internet exposure counts to reduce false positives in vulnerability surface metrics.
Distribution model that bundles detection artifacts via git SSH repos and encrypted/offline backups for air-gapped or higher-security consumption.
